transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Oh, no.
Can you hear me?
There we go.
I I can hear you now.
Hi there, Samantha.
I'm so sorry.
That's okay.
I'm little super familiar with the meetings.
I was trying to find a room, and then I did grab a room, and then I have AirPods in.
And so,
It like, in the open room, being very loud.
All right.
Well, nice to meet you.
Thank you.
I'm so sorry.
I'm so late.
But I'm very excited to chat with you.
You have so far, who have you talked to so far?
Is it Mayla?
Marilia?
Yeah, Marilia.
Yes.
Okay, great.
So you just got some context for Marilia.
And where are you based?
I'm based in Pittsburgh.
Awesome.
Cool.
I am also usually on the East Coast, but I'm in SF this week.
Cool.
So I can like just give you a bit of a sense of the role and kind of how we're thinking about it, how it fits in the growthx kind of picture.
And then we can spend most of the time talking about you.
These definitely do not take 45 minutes.
So like, we will not be short on time.
I think I have like four.
Questions that I normally, that I'm set to ask you.
So we should be good there.
Cool.
I know I'm a little hectic jumping in here, but so I lead customer operations at GrowthX.
We've been around for a little more than a year.
So we're at the stage where like renewing our first batch of customers and, you know, thinking a lot about kind of scalability of the services side of the business.

So we are today set up as an agency model, traditional pod structure where it's kind of like a, we have engagement managers who manage a book of like eight-ish clients today, probably scaling to 12 sometime, maybe like around Q2 of next year.
Um, and, and then they have, um, managing editors who own production and to end for three.
that's even have many other studies.
Thank
So there are three managing editors that are kind of running content production on our AI workflows.
So we have an internal platform that leverages AI to generate content.
It's getting better and better every day.
So the goal is to get it so that it's like 10 to 15 minutes for editing an article.
There's a range of that.
Some topics were more successful at hitting that goal, but that's the aim.
There is a world in the future where part of that platform becomes external facing, and then we have like a services side and a product side.
But today, very traditional agency model.
So this role, yeah, it's really relationship focused and thinking about like strategy.
Performance for the client.
A little bit of project management.
So you're not going to be editing content or, you know, like thinking about like the sort of like day-to-day content production that will be like the managing editors.

But it is, you'll be kind of like communicating with clients in Slack, keeping them updated on kind of project status, and also talking to them about like performance against goals on a week-over-week basis.

In terms of like longer-term roadmap, higher-level strategy with clients, we do have a pool of like director-level SEO-type specialists who can hop in and help on a quarterly basis, like put together QBRs, think about like strategic shifts in focus, because, you know, content strategy is constantly changing.
So those sorts of things.
So, yeah, this is really, like, I think relationship and renewal is, like, the main KPIs that you'll own as an engagement manager.
And that's through, like, tracking performance and making sure that we're kind of all aligned in the direction that we're going.
Any questions?
No, I don't think so.
Not right now.
Yeah, kind of anything that I was still, like, slightly curious about, you pretty much just answered.
So that's good.
Yeah, perfect.
Perfect.
Great.
Well, I'd love to learn a little bit about you.
So, like a lot of other people, I definitely fell into, like, the customer success or, you know, customer realm somewhat on accident.
I was a hairstylist for 12 years, right?
So I was always integrated with people, learning about them.
And then I got into the digital landscape.
And, you know, my first shot was actually, I got really far away from it.
And I was just...
was...
Like event coordination and media coordination and website things.
And I don't think I talked to anybody for a couple of years.
And I was like, I can't do this.
So I got back into, you know, being more customer facing.
And I started with a company here in Pittsburgh.
They were really small at the time.
There was maybe 10 of us total on the team.
We built out kind of how we would work.
Like you said, kind of, you know, a lot of them came from agencies.
So at first, like that whole, we all, we need like these little pods where, you know, that's how everything started there.
And we would all work together and collaborate in a group.

My main role there was, you know, kind of walking through the customers with what projects they were doing, how they wanted to see their future, what kind of metrics were important to them, and how, you know, they truthfully would understand value with, you know, what we were presenting.
And after doing that for a little bit, I was brought.
Brought on to BrightEdge, and I really just expanded all that from there, right?

And I learned a little bit more about like how, you know, helping people to see value and really walking through those pain points and discovery with them was, you know, ultimately how you're going to see those renewals and see those expansions, because then you can call those things out early on.
So I've been at BrightEdge for a while now.
I'm a senior customer success manager for them.
But I feel kind of like stalled in, you know, where I'm at.
So I've, you know, it was just by having that, you know, you guys have reached out to me and I was like, well, I am looking.
So I'd be more than happy.
Yeah.
Amazing.
Can you talk to me a little bit about like the AI assisted adoption playbooks and like the AI education programs that you've put together?
Yeah, I think for AI, because it's so rapid changing, the real key to it is getting somebody into like.
Like implementing a workflow.
Right.
And like I mentioned, it's, it's on their level.

So you have to understand like where they're seeing either issues or where they're comfortable working and how you can, you know, walk them through the process of implementing something into like every day.
Right.
So I think that discovery has been super important here.
You know, I do have customers right now who are above and beyond.
Right.
They understand that to, you know, really put out good content.
They need to have some of that expertise behind them.
They need to have some of that authority behind them.
And they need to really understand their business and their topics.
You know, we talk about, you know, discuss things like that.
And, you know, so we have an AI product.
We have a couple of AI products that do.
They do some content creation.
They do some research.
Right.
Right.
So we try to couple those together to just, you know, like walk them through that.

And like I said, I think a lot of it is just getting them comfortable, training them on what to do and how to do it, making them demo it for you so you know that they're comfortable, and then getting their feedback and kind of starting that loop over again to make sure that they're in a good spot.
Awesome.
Cool.
Okay.
Can you talk to me about an account that you manage that requires support from like different teams across your company and how you coordinate that?
Yeah.
So I currently work with a customer who's in call it the communications industry.
And they require they do require a lot of support.
They came from an agency.
So we do have them connect quite frequently with like engineering and development, because they don't know a lot of like back end and development tools.
You know, so immediately with that, we made sure that they were comfortable with our development team.
Right.
Introduced them, but did make it very clear cut that, you know, like I am your main point of contact.
So, you know, trust me to tell me anything and I'll tell you if this is the right next step or where we need to go.
They also have had to work with the product team.
Right.
So being someone that's come from an area where they were with an agency and now they're on their own, you know, we've kind of worked with them to get a lot of feedback as well.
And when it comes to that, you know, I've clearly been their main point of contact for the product team.
You know, we don't necessarily call out like, hey, that's who you're working with.
But, you know, I've kind of told them like, hey, I've passed along a lot of this information.

So, you know, for your pain points and where you're seeing issues, we can go ahead and, you know, either expand for you or try to work on, you know, just getting you just more comfortable in a specific area.
Yeah.
Cool.
Cool.
And then talk to me about like, as you're thinking through deliverables or.
Or working through that kind of internal piece, how you make sure that the work stays organized and you're staying on track.
And the team is staying on track also, like I think that coordination piece.
So like, I like to call out a couple of different things.
And so when I'm looking at my accounts that I'm meeting with, either that week or even up and coming the next week, right, we like to look at things like who is high impact?
You know, are we talking about just high priority customers?
Are we talking about people who are at risk of churn, people who are up for renewal, things like that?
Customers that might need intervention and just those regular check-ins, right?
And kind of communicating that with my team at the beginning of the week, right?
Whether I'm working with, like I said, product engineering or even our account management team for, you know, like, hey, who you need to send a contract to.
And kind of, you know, establishing that, sending out like, hey, these are going to be our priorities.
And then from there.
You know, getting any feedback from them, anything they might need on their end that I need to address.
And, you know, of course, taking that back to the customer.
But I think, you know, I'm always looking for those health signals and things like that from the customer perspective so that my team isn't digging through a lot of things, right?
Like anybody that I'm working with internally, I want to make sure that that's as seamless for you.
And I'm just coming to you, either asking you for either a solution or like a document, whatever I need.
And that's it.
And, you know, we can go forward from there.
So like I said, it's seamless for the customer.
The customer doesn't have to see all that.
And it's seamless for the team.
Yeah.
Just taking notes.
Yeah.
No problem.
All right.
And then what like tools are you using to stay organized?
Project management pieces, things like that.
So in the past, I've used Asana, I've used Monday.
Currently at BrightEdge, we do have our own kind of like management system that they built out for us.
It's called Yoda.
I feel like it's fairly similar to Asana.
You know, so it connects us all internally.
Everyone can look at tickets, look at notes, look at progress so they can see what's going on with the customer.
We do use Gainsight, we use Salesforce, and of course, like DealDesk for support tickets.
So, you know, there is a great, you know, interconnectivity.
There's so many places that you can check to kind of see what's going on.
But the main hub, like I said, is this, it's called Yoda.
And that's where like the notes and everything like that live.
And of course, we use Teams like for immediate or, you know, kind of any communication that needs to happen right away.
Got it.
So, how are you primarily communicating with clients today?
Um.
I'm.
With clients, mostly through either email or video, there are a few customers who I know who kind of, you know, prefer that quick answer.
So I do call people still, which I feel like it's so rare, but most of my communication is either video or email.
Awesome.
Yeah, we're big Slack.
We have Slack channel for every client, which I have mixed feelings about, but it is a fast way to get answers.
And so it's like a good, seamless communication, though it does require a lot of juggling.
Yeah, wasn't exactly a direct online marketing.
We had a Slack channel for everybody.
And I mean, it's nice because if there is something that they do need taken care of, it's there.
Also, I mean, you know, you probably work with Outlook and it's not the easiest to sort through.
If you don't like pre-organize everything, it's really hard to find emails and go back to them.
Yeah, yeah.
What was it?
Oh, yeah.
What do you think about Gainsight?
We're like evaluating options for different tools and stuff.
And I'm, you know, just curious about your take on Gainsight.
Gainsight's good in certain ways.
I mean, I like, you know, for how we use it, I like that I can really see like exactly what everybody's doing, right?

So if we're trying to get customers on board of the workflow or, you know, they don't want to admit that they're struggling, but clearly when you're in sessions with them or talking with them and they keep asking you like the same thing and they're getting hung up on things, you know, or even they're like, oh, I wasn't able to make progress.
You can actually see what they're, you know, like you can see what they're doing.
So I like that, right?

Because then I can be like, seems like maybe we need to focus on this area more without specifically calling out like, hey, you know, obviously, you know, you don't want to say you have a problem there.
So we'll just work around that.
Some of the things like, I feel like it's always
And Gainsai, just like everything else, I feel like it's always lagging, though.
Like, it never works seamlessly with other tools.
Yeah, yeah.
Nothing does.
Okay, last question.
Can you talk to me about a time when a client deliverable was going off track, and what you had to do to get them back?
Absolutely.
I feel like with so many clients, this is something, especially right now, that is happening a lot.
Everybody wants to press forward into that whole AI sphere.
So, for example, I have one customer, and we had just redone all of the pillar pages on their website.
And really, the goal was then from there to go down to each segment and page, update some of your basic SEO features, and then update your content on that page.
And this was something we had started as our strategy for 2025.
system goes for me by system out Bye.
Because we really wanted to see at the end of 2025 how much impact we have made there.
Of course, like I said, with everything AI coming onto the scene, they got a little deterred and they were like, we're going to start writing content on this.
And I'm like, okay, that's fantastic.
Let's see how many ways that we can make it relate back.
And again, too, I think it's really just about building that muscle memory with a customer, right?
Because what happens when you get off track is you don't have that daily workflow.
So then you feel flustered when you do have to work on something.
Also, I can't provide that value for you at the end of a quarter, at the end of the year, whatever it happens to be, because we've derailed.
So, you know, making sure that you can tie those projects in and making your customer understand like, hey, I know that this is a priority for you.
And I'm well aware that it is very important, but we can make this all work together, right?
And you just want to keep on that consistent workflow.
So we're not losing progress anywhere.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Have you ever had to, like, you know, do you do any org mapping or thinking about, like, expansions and making sure that you have the right people in the room?
Do you have an approach that you take to keep, especially, like, DRIs or budget holders, like, engaged?
I think something that we see sometimes is engaged, like, the person that had the budget, got really excited.
Like, you know, we have really, like, a really strong sales team, and our CEO is also often involved in the sales process.
So he will, like, sign deals with CMOs, but the CMO is not our day-to-day contact.
So wondering, like, what your take is on kind of keeping those, like, higher-level people engaged and interested in the work that you're doing so that we don't lose kind of that buy-in.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
I have a
They're in the manufacturing industry.
And as you just mentioned, their CEO, he's the one that signed with us originally.
He has champed us through and through.
He just speaks so highly of us.
However, he's not on the day-to calls.
He's not on the day-to-day projects, anything like that.
To be honest, usually I hear from him once a year when he's like, hey, what are the projects that we did?
However, even though I only hear back from him once a year, I do keep him engaged.
He is on every single follow-up.
He gets recordings from every time that we do a review, that we go over metrics, anything like that.
And then, of course, when we talk about strategy or when there's something new like product releases, everything, we try to make sure that we're in contact with him as well.
My account team typically hears from him more than I do.
Like I said, I get that once a year, like, hey, just give me some numbers.
But he will tell us, you know, like in return, like, Hey, this was great to see or anything like that.
And I do try to just make sure, of course, with my day to day contact, right?
I'm always like, is this something that needs approval from Andy, right?
Like, that's our go to, like, no, you know, just so that she knows that I am keeping him in the loop as well.
So I think it helps keep the customer on top of things as well.
Awesome.
Those were all the questions I had.
What questions do you have for me?
I think mostly, like, some of the ones that you answered at the beginning, you know, how do the teams integrate?
So that was pretty clear.
Other than, like, retention, of course, expansion, your typical ones, what kind of impact do you guys try to share with your customer or, you know, hope to see from your customers beyond that?
Yeah, so I think, like, our customers are thinking about, like, engagement and conversion.
So, like, what we're...
Tracking classically is, like, kind of traffic and, like, clicks and, like, just, like, site visits.
But what we're starting to talk more about is AI visibility.
We have a new site that's, like, going to go live very soon.
And that's, like, I think a very direct funnel that we're starting to push.
I mean, I'm sure you know all about that in the work that you're doing.
But, like, you know, LLM referrals is a huge new opportunity.
And so, like, trying to tie back, like, referrals and get mentions up, not citations, but actual, like, mentions and inclusions and AI answers is, like, a high priority.
Because that's, I think, like, the biggest opportunity for click-throughs and conversions.
We're not, I think we need to be doing better at, like, tracking the pipeline conversions.
But, you know, I think.

Contribution's always a mess and really hard to, like, get to the bottom of, but, like, the, I think that's, like, something that, that we'll start to hone in on over time, but, like, that AI visibility piece is something that we talk a lot about right now.
Okay.
Other than that, I think, you know, for this particular role, like, six to 12 months from now, what do you see as success there?

Yeah, I mean, I think the directions that this role could go are really interesting, like, depending on when we launch the product and kind of the way that we develop our, like, the direction that the company goes in generally.
I think, like, there is, like, a sort of product SaaS play, and you, you could be owning, like, a side of that business, but I think in terms of, like, growing and success.

And this role specifically, like if we were just to continue sort of linearly as we are, it would be thinking about like having, owning a book that's like a true sort of enterprise level engagement with having like grown clients.
And also like a measurement of success is really going to be around like AI curiosity and the ability to help us like define and scope new work streams for clients.
So, you know, right now we're, our book is growing just like because we're taking on so many new clients.
But I think like there's a lot of opportunity for us to build new pipelines, new like offerings and expand contracts that way.
But I think the engagement managers are going to be really key in defining like what that looks like and what's sustainable.
I mean, like long, long term, that could even mean like owning like that work stream and definition and helping expand contracts across businesses.
But, like, adding work streams, I think, is, like, a big opportunity and something that, like, we need help defining, and an engagement manager can play a really core role in.
Okay.
All right.
Sounds good.
What brought you to work here?

Um, so I, uh, I, I, they found me, um, similar to you, but I think, like, the thing that I find most exciting about growthx, like, this is my second Series A, uh, in a row, and, um, my last Series A, like, was traditional product, SaaS, like, um, and it was very much, like, subscription or die, like, because we're built on the services muscle, um, we have, like, a lot more runway, and, like, our growth is not contingent on, like, the product launching and being successful on day one, um, and so I think it's, like, giving our engineering team an opportunity.
To kind of like blue sky a lot of things that is gonna that makes like, if you could see what we're building on the back end, it's like really exciting, it's going to be super compelling.
And we don't have that like, pressure of like, launch it so that we can make money.

I mean, like, obviously, we have we like, that's a consideration, but like the services side of the business is, is like running and keeping it like running itself and that like human, piece of it is really core to what we do.
And I think like, we'll continue to be even as we grow, like, the services side is, is super valuable.
And the work that we're doing is like, really hard to replicate, although some clients think that they can do it themselves, as always.
But yeah, like, I think like that, that we started with the services muscle and are building on the product side.
And so we're like, literally dogfooding our own product offering is really cool.
Okay, that's fantastic.
That's amazing to hear, actually.
All right.
Yeah, it's very comforting.
Yeah, and it's like a very different response, I guess, than, you know, kind of what I've ever heard.
All right.
That's really it.
Yeah, that's the only questions that I have.
Awesome.
Well, like I said, definitely not a 45-minute call.
I'm so sorry that I was late, but it was really great chatting with you.
So, and yeah, we'll talk to you soon.
Okay.
Thank you so much.
Thanks, Samantha.
Bye.
